key_insight: Modularity only helps continual learning when the representation is sufficiently low-dimensional; in high-dimensional lazy regimes, modular and single-network models perform similarly.
engineering_relevance: For compositional systems, the benefit of architectural modularity depends on representational regime, suggesting designers should tune initialization and capacity to encourage useful task subspaces rather than relying on hard separation alone.
method: Compared a task-partitioned recurrent network against a single-network baseline
Manipulated weight scale to induce high- and low-dimensional representational regimes
evidence_signal: The abstract reports performance and internal geometry comparisons across similar, moderately dissimilar, and dissimilar tasks, showing modular subspaces overlap, align, or separate depending on task similarity in the rich regime.
watch_item: The conclusions are tied to a specific sequential task setup and weight-scale-induced regime, so results may not transfer directly to other architectures or more complex real-world continual learning settings.
paper_title: Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Helps in Continual Learning
